questions.specification: IB Sports, Exercise and Health Science (SEHS) - Motor Learning and Fitts and Posner Model

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on stages of learning (Fitts & Posner), characteristics of the associative stage, differences between cognitive and autonomous stages, and how practice structure supports skill acquisition—core motor learning process concepts.

parts.content: Identify two stages of Fitts and Posner’s model of learning.
parts.markscheme:
● Identify the cognitive stage <marks>A1</marks>
● Identify the associative stage <marks>A1</marks>
<note>Other acceptable answer: Autonomous stage. Award 1 mark for each stage identified up to 2 marks.</note>
[2 marks]

parts.content: State two characteristics of the associative stage of learning.
parts.markscheme:
● State that there are fewer and less pronounced errors <marks>A1</marks>
● State that there is improved movement coordination <marks>A1</marks>
<note>Other acceptable answers include: refinement of technique, increased consistency, requires less cognitive effort.</note>
[2 marks]

parts.content: Describe how feedback supports motor learning.
parts.markscheme:
● Describe the provision of knowledge of results (KR) or knowledge of performance (KP) <marks>A1</marks>
● Describe how feedback reinforces correct technique or behavior <marks>A1</marks>
● Describe how feedback guides error correction and the modification of movement <marks>A1</marks>
[3 marks]

parts.content: Explain how the cognitive stage differs from the autonomous stage in terms of performance.
parts.markscheme:
● Explain that the cognitive stage is characterized by a high error rate and inconsistent performance <marks>A1</marks>
● Explain that the cognitive stage requires high levels of conscious effort and concentration <marks>A1</marks>
● Explain that in the autonomous stage, movements are smooth, fluid, and automatic <marks>A1</marks>
● Explain that the autonomous stage requires minimal conscious thought, allowing for multitasking <marks>A1</marks>
[4 marks]

parts.content: Outline the role of distributed practice in learning new motor skills.
parts.markscheme:
● Outline that practice sessions are broken into shorter segments interspersed with rest intervals <marks>A1</marks>
● Outline how this allows for the consolidation of learning and information <marks>A1</marks>
● Outline how it reduces physical and mental fatigue, improving practice quality <marks>A1</marks>
● Outline how it enhances long-term retention of the skill <marks>A1</marks>
[4 marks]

parts.content: Discuss how a coach could structure a practice session for a beginner athlete based on the cognitive stage.
parts.markscheme:
● Discuss providing clear visual demonstrations and verbal instructions to establish the motor program <marks>A1</marks>
● Discuss using blocked or repetitive practice to reduce cognitive load and build basic mechanics <marks>A1</marks>
● Discuss offering frequent extrinsic feedback (knowledge of performance and results) to guide learning <marks>A1</marks>
● Discuss allowing sufficient time for trial and error, repetition, and asking questions <marks>A1</marks>
● Discuss keeping tasks simple and relevant to prevent information overload for the athlete <marks>A1</marks>
[5 marks]

questions.specification: The question assesses understanding of motor learning, stages of learning, types of feedback, and practice methods in sports science.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is centered on motor learning concepts (feedback, stages of learning, practice methods) and how they contribute to skill acquisition, aligning with learning as a process producing relatively permanent change.

parts.content: Identify two types of feedback used in motor learning.
parts.markscheme:
- Intrinsic feedback <marks>1</marks>
- Extrinsic feedback <marks>1</marks>

<note>(Other acceptable answers: Knowledge of results, Knowledge of performance — any two)</note>

[2 marks]

parts.content: State two features of the autonomous stage of learning.
parts.markscheme:
- Movements are automatic and require little conscious thought <marks>1</marks>
- High level of consistency and accuracy <marks>1</marks>

<note>(Other acceptable answers: Able to focus on tactics or strategy, Performs well under pressure — any two)</note>

[2 marks]

parts.content: Describe how intrinsic feedback aids skill acquisition.
parts.markscheme:
- Feedback from within the body <marks>1</marks>
- Encourages self-awareness and correction <marks>1</marks>
- Enhances independent learning <marks>1</marks>

[3 marks]

parts.content: Explain the importance of knowledge of performance (KP) in refining technique.
parts.markscheme:
- KP focuses on movement technique <marks>1</marks>
- Helps correct form during practice <marks>1</marks>
- More effective in refining complex motor patterns <marks>1</marks>
- Reinforces efficient movement <marks>1</marks>

[4 marks]

parts.content: Outline the differences between massed and distributed practice.
parts.markscheme:
- Massed: long sessions, minimal rest <marks>1</marks>
- Distributed: shorter sessions with rest <marks>1</marks>
- Distributed preferred for beginners <marks>1</marks>
- Massed may lead to fatigue <marks>1</marks>

[4 marks]

parts.content: Discuss how feedback frequency should be adjusted throughout the stages of learning.
parts.markscheme:
- Frequent feedback for beginners enhances learning <marks>1</marks>
- Reduced frequency builds independence <marks>1</marks>
- Too much feedback causes dependence <marks>1</marks>
- Advanced learners benefit from less frequent, precise feedback <marks>1</marks>
- Stage-specific adjustment improves retention <marks>1</marks>

[5 marks]

questions.specification:
A sport scientist monitors a professional sprinter's start and records the following performance data:

| Event Sequence | Time ($$s$$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| From the starter's gun until the first change in pressure on the blocks | $$0.18$$ |
| From the first change in pressure until the feet leave the blocks | $$0.13$$ |

Based on this information, what is the reaction time for this athlete?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.2 The psychological refractory period and its impact on reaction time
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.2 The psychological refractory period and its impact on reaction time
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the athlete’s reaction time from the starter’s gun to the first detectable movement (change in pressure on the blocks), which is a reaction-time measurement concept addressed under psychological refractory period and reaction time.

options.content: $$0.05$$ seconds
$$0.13$$ seconds
$$0.18$$ seconds
$$0.31$$ seconds

questions.specification: Why is the distinction between learning and performance important for coaches?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about distinguishing learning (relatively permanent change) from performance (temporary fluctuations), which is central to defining learning in motor learning and avoiding misattributing short-term performance changes to long-term learning.

options.content: To avoid misinterpreting short-term performance changes as long-term learning
options.markscheme: Correct: learning is a relatively permanent change, while performance can vary temporarily due to factors such as fatigue, motivation, or arousal.

options.content: To ensure that improvements seen during practice are retained and transferred to competition
options.markscheme: Incorrect: retention and transfer relate to learning, but this does not explain why learning and performance must be distinguished (performance during practice may not reflect learning).

options.content: Because immediate performance is always a reliable indicator of learning
options.markscheme: Incorrect: immediate performance can be influenced by temporary factors and may not indicate a relatively permanent change in capability.

options.content: Because learning cannot be directly observed and must be inferred from performance over time
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this is true, but it does not capture the key coaching implication as directly as option A (avoiding misinterpretation of short-term performance as learning).

questions.specification:
An athlete’s performance scores are recorded across two basketball free-throw training periods, as shown in the table below.

| Training Period | Trial 1 | Trial 2 | Trial 3 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Week 1 | 25% | 22% | 24% |
| Week 6 | 85% | 88% | 86% |

The consistent improvement observed between the first and last week is evidence of:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks what consistent improvement in free-throw performance over weeks indicates; this aligns with learning defined as a relatively permanent change in performance/behavior due to practice (motor learning).

options.content: Performance plateau
options.markscheme: Incorrect: a plateau would show little or no change in performance over time, not a large consistent improvement.

options.content: Learning
options.markscheme: Correct: the marked improvement from Week 1 to Week 6 indicates improvement due to practice/skill acquisition (learning).

options.content: Maturation
options.markscheme: Incorrect: maturation refers to changes due to growth/ageing rather than improvement primarily attributable to practice across training sessions.

options.content: Selective attention
options.markscheme: Incorrect: selective attention is a focus mechanism and does not by itself best explain the overall consistent long-term improvement shown in the trials.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.2 Attentional control > C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Multiple parts focus on attentional focus/control, cueing to enhance attention, and maintaining attentional control under high-stakes performance—core elements of attentional control in motor learning.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Blocked vs random practice effects on memory retention are practice-structure effects within motor learning, linking to how learning produces relatively permanent changes and retention.

parts.content: Identify two forms of augmented feedback.
parts.markscheme:
- knowledge of results <marks>A1</marks>
- knowledge of performance <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each form identified. Other acceptable answers include verbal feedback and video replay.</note>

parts.content: State two types of cues used to enhance attention.
parts.markscheme:
- visual cues <marks>A1</marks>
- verbal cues <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each type stated. Other acceptable answers include auditory cues and proprioceptive cues.</note>

parts.content: Describe the impact of blocked vs. random practice on memory retention.
parts.markscheme:
- blocked practice: associated with better short-term performance <marks>A1</marks>
- random practice: improves long-term retention of skills <marks>A1</marks>
- random practice: creates stronger and more diverse memory links <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Explain how attentional focus strategies improve skill execution.
parts.markscheme:
- keeps attention on relevant aspects of the task while filtering out irrelevant stimuli <marks>A1</marks>
- enhances motor pattern accuracy by focusing on specific technical cues <marks>A1</marks>
- reduces error from distraction by maintaining a narrow focus when necessary <marks>A1</marks>
- enables better anticipation by recognizing environmental patterns earlier <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Outline how visual and verbal cues can be integrated into coaching.
parts.markscheme:
- visual cues: using demonstrations or video playback to model correct technique <marks>A1</marks>
- verbal cues: using brief, actionable phrases to trigger specific movements <marks>A1</marks>
- integration: combining both to reinforce understanding through different sensory modalities <marks>A1</marks>
- clarity: improves clarity for learners when executing complex multi-stage tasks <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Discuss the importance of attentional control during high-stakes performance.
parts.markscheme:
- helps the athlete resist distraction under high levels of stress or pressure <marks>A1</marks>
- ensures attention remains on key information/task-relevant cues rather than the crowd or score <marks>A1</marks>
- maintains competitive routine and mental preparation during breaks in play <marks>A1</marks>
- supports decision-making accuracy by preventing "paralysis by analysis" or cognitive overload <marks>A1</marks>
- critical for maintaining confidence by focusing on process goals rather than outcome <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

questions.specification: The psychological refractory period occurs when:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.2 The psychological refractory period and its impact on reaction time
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.2 The psychological refractory period and its impact on reaction time
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the definition of the psychological refractory period—when processing the first stimulus delays the response to a second—matching the motor learning subtopic on PRP and reaction time.

options.content: An athlete responds more quickly to the second stimulus than to the first
options.markscheme: Incorrect. In the psychological refractory period, the second response is typically slowed, not speeded up.

options.content: The response to a second stimulus is delayed while the first is being processed
options.markscheme: Correct. When two stimuli occur close together, processing the first limits capacity and delays the response to the second.

options.content: The response to a second stimulus is delayed because attention/processing capacity is limited when stimuli occur close together
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This describes the same mechanism as the correct answer but is not the accepted defining phrasing here; the key point is that the second response is delayed while the first is processed.

options.content: The response to the first stimulus is delayed because the second stimulus is prioritised
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The typical effect is a delay to the response to the second stimulus, not the first.

questions.specification: According to Fitts and Posner, which stage of motor learning is characterized by a high frequency of gross errors and a heavy reliance on extrinsic feedback?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about stages of motor learning (cognitive/associative/autonomous) and characteristics like gross errors and reliance on extrinsic feedback, which falls under general motor learning processes.

options.content: Cognitive stage
options.markscheme: Correct: In the cognitive stage learners make frequent gross errors and rely heavily on extrinsic feedback.

options.content: Associative stage
options.markscheme: Incorrect: In the associative stage errors are less frequent and feedback needs decrease as movements become more refined.

options.content: Autonomous stage
options.markscheme: Incorrect: In the autonomous stage performance is consistent with few errors and minimal reliance on extrinsic feedback.

options.content: Cognitive–associative stage
options.markscheme: Incorrect: Fitts and Posner describe three distinct stages (cognitive, associative, autonomous), not a combined cognitive–associative stage.

questions.specification: In which scenario would the psychological refractory period most likely reduce performance?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.2 The psychological refractory period and its impact on reaction time
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.2 The psychological refractory period and its impact on reaction time
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks about the psychological refractory period (PRP) reducing performance, which is a motor learning process affecting reaction time—especially in tasks requiring two rapid successive responses (e.g., goalkeeper facing quick rebound shots).

options.content: A goalkeeper reacting to two quick rebound shots in succession
options.markscheme: Correct: A

options.content: A marathon runner pacing steadily for 2 hours
options.markscheme: Incorrect: the task does not involve rapid successive stimulus–response selections where PRP would most affect performance.

options.content: A cyclist maintaining a consistent cadence
options.markscheme: Incorrect: steady-state rhythmic movement involves minimal rapid serial decision-making, so PRP effects are unlikely to be primary.

options.content: A weightlifter attempting a one-rep max
options.markscheme: Incorrect: performance is primarily limited by strength and technique rather than rapid processing of two successive stimuli/responses.

questions.specification:
The table shows the putting accuracy of a professional golfer in two different settings:

| Environment | Success Rate |
| :--- | :--- |
| Training Session | $94 \%$ |
| Major Championship | $68 \%$ |

Which statement explains the decrease in accuracy during the championship, based on the distinction between motor learning and performance?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question explicitly asks for the motor learning vs performance distinction, emphasizing learning as a relatively permanent change while performance is temporary/variable due to situational factors (pressure/competition).

options.content: Performance is an unstable, observable behavior that is influenced by situational factors, whereas learning is a more permanent underlying change.
options.markscheme: Correct: describes that performance can fluctuate with pressure/anxiety/environmental factors, while learning is relatively permanent.

options.content: Learning is a temporary state of improved accuracy, while performance is the consistent ability to repeat a skill under pressure.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: reverses the definitions (learning is relatively permanent; performance is temporary/variable).

options.content: The decrease in accuracy suggests that the golfer has regressed from the autonomous stage to the cognitive stage of learning due to the crowd.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: performance can decline under pressure without a true regression in learning stage.

options.content: The lower championship success rate shows that the golfer’s underlying motor learning was not fully permanent, but their performance can still vary from one setting to another because of situational factors.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: although it mentions situational variation, it incorrectly concludes the underlying learning was not permanent solely from a single performance drop.

questions.specification: A sprinter uses their starting block experience to improve their swimming dive start. This is an example of:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario describes prior experience in one skill (starting blocks in sprinting) improving performance in a similar skill (swimming dive start), which is transfer of learning—specifically positive transfer.

options.content: Negative transfer of learning
Temporary performance improvement
Positive transfer of learning
No relationship between skills

questions.specification: A basketball player learning free throws more quickly because of their experience with netball is an example of:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario describes prior experience in a similar sport (netball) helping acquisition of a basketball skill (free throws), which is a classic example of transfer of learning.

options.content: Psychological refractory period
Transfer of learning
Temporary performance change
Positive transfer

questions.specification: Why can performance improvements in training sessions be misleading?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question contrasts apparent performance improvements in practice with true learning, emphasizing that learning is a relatively permanent change while practice performance can be temporarily influenced by factors like motivation, fatigue, and environment.

options.content: They always indicate permanent learning.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: performance gains can be temporary and not reflect true learning.

options.content: They may reflect temporary factors such as motivation, fatigue, or environment.
options.markscheme: Correct: short-term performance changes can be influenced by temporary factors rather than relatively permanent learning.

options.content: They can result from dependence on feedback or guidance that is later removed.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this can make practice performance appear better than learning (e.g., guidance hypothesis), but it is not the best general reason compared with temporary factors overall.

options.content: They prove that transfer of learning has not occurred.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: improvements do not prove anything about (lack of) transfer; transfer must be assessed directly in new contexts/tasks.

questions.specification: Which term describes a relatively permanent change in the capability to perform a skill, shown by improved basketball shooting accuracy after months of practice?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question describes a relatively permanent improvement in skill (shooting accuracy) after extended practice, which matches the definition of motor learning as a process leading to relatively permanent behavioral change.

options.content: Performance
Motor learning
Mood change
Fatigue recovery

questions.specification: A basketball player misses two free throws in practice but makes them consistently in games. What does this suggest about their motor learning?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario contrasts practice vs game performance, highlighting that performance can vary moment-to-moment while underlying learning (relatively permanent change) may still be present.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It implicitly involves applying a practiced skill in a different context (practice to competition), touching on transfer/application across settings, though the main point is performance vs learning.

options.content: Their performance fluctuates, but learning has occurred
No learning has taken place
They have not transferred skills to competition
They only learn under pressure

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts ask about stages of learning (associative and autonomous) and general motor learning concepts such as how practice and motivation influence learning—core motor learning process content.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.3 Motivation > C.3.2 Self-determination > C.3.2.2 Motivation continuum: from amotivation to controlled and autonomous motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.2.2 Motivation continuum: from amotivation to controlled and autonomous motivation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) focuses on motivation’s influence on motor learning, aligning with motivation types/continuum that can affect persistence, effort, and learning outcomes.

parts.content: Identify two types of practice used to develop motor skills.

- Identify random practice <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how coaches can support progression from associative to autonomous stage.

questions.specification: Which scenario demonstrates positive transfer of learning?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which scenario shows positive transfer of learning—improvement in one skill due to practice of another—directly matching the motor learning subtopic on transfer of learning.

options.content: A rugby player’s tackling skills improve after practicing judo throws.
options.markscheme: Correct: A — improvement in a skill due to practice of a related skill is positive transfer.

options.content: A tennis player loses accuracy in badminton due to different wrist actions.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this describes negative transfer (performance worsens due to interference between similar skills).

options.content: A sprinter’s times vary due to stress and fatigue.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this describes performance variability due to psychological/physiological factors, not transfer of learning.

options.content: A swimmer’s reaction is delayed by processing a false start.
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this describes a reaction-time/decision-processing effect, not transfer of learning.

questions.specification: Which is the best sporting example of negative transfer of learning?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for an example of negative transfer of learning, which is explicitly part of transfer of learning within motor learning and skill acquisition.

options.content: A tennis player struggles to adapt to badminton wrist movements
A basketball player improving free throws through netball
A sprinter improving starts from previous swimming dives
A gymnast learning balance skills from dance

questions.specification: Which statement best describes this scenario: a soccer player’s penalty accuracy improves over a season, but in one match they miss a penalty due to performance anxiety?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario contrasts long-term improvement (learning as a relatively permanent change) with a single poor outcome, highlighting the learning vs performance distinction—performance can vary despite learning.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.4 Stress and coping > C.4.1 Arousal and anxiety > C.4.1.2 Anxiety's dual effect
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.1.2 Anxiety's dual effect
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Missing due to nerves points to anxiety affecting performance, which can temporarily disrupt skilled execution despite underlying learning.

options.content: The player has not learned the skill.
Performance can fluctuate despite learning.
Temporary psychological factors (such as anxiety) can impair performance without preventing learning.
The skill has not been retained.

questions.specification: Which of the following is an example of performance fluctuation rather than learning?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question contrasts temporary performance fluctuations (e.g., day-to-day variability due to fatigue) with learning, defined as a relatively permanent change in behavior/skill.

options.content: A runner’s sprint time varies daily depending on fatigue
options.markscheme: A

options.content: A gymnast masters a new skill after weeks of practice
options.markscheme: Learning: relatively permanent improvement due to practice.

options.content: A tennis player adapts a serve from volleyball experience
options.markscheme: Learning/transfer of learning from previous experience.

options.content: A swimmer permanently improves breathing technique
options.markscheme: Learning: relatively permanent improvement in technique.

questions.specification:
The table displays an athlete’s experience when transitioning between two different sports.

| Years of Practice | Initial Sport | New Sport | Performance Observation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 5 years | Squash | Tennis | Previous habits with a flexible wrist interfere with the required firm-wrist technique for volleys. |

Which type of transfer of learning is illustrated in this scenario?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario describes prior skill habits from squash (flexible wrist) interfering with learning/performing a tennis volley technique (firm wrist), which is classic negative transfer within the broader concept of transfer of learning.

options.content: Positive transfer
Zero transfer
Negative transfer
Bilateral transfer

questions.specification: A soccer player’s penalty kick accuracy improves during training sessions but does not improve in matches. This outcome reflects a lack of:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario contrasts improved accuracy in practice but not in competition, highlighting the distinction between relatively permanent learning and temporary performance effects; improvement only in training suggests it is not true learning.

options.content: Learning
Transfer of learning
Performance
Psychological refractory period

questions.specification:
A basketball point guard is bringing the ball up the court in the final 10 seconds of a tied game. The crowd is loud, defenders are applying pressure, and the coach is calling instructions from the sideline. A teammate signals for a pass while another teammate sets a screen.

Table 1 shows changes in attention at different arousal levels for this athlete.

| Arousal level | Typical attentional focus | Common performance effect |
|---|---|---|
| Low | Broad/external scanning | Misses key cues; slow reactions |
| Moderate | Appropriate breadth; relevant cues prioritized | Accurate decisions and timing |
| High | Narrowed focus (“tunnel vision”) | Misses open teammates; forced errors |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.2 Attentional control > C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on attentional focus/types, selective attention, cue utilization, managing distractions, and how arousal impacts attentional control—core elements of attentional control in motor performance.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.4 Stress and coping > C.4.1 Arousal and anxiety > C.4.1.1 Arousal as a physical and psychological activation affecting performance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.1.1 Arousal as a physical and psychological activation affecting performance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (e) specifically links arousal level to attentional control, aligning with arousal’s effects on performance and attentional narrowing/broadening.

parts.content: Identify two types of attention relevant to motor performance in sport.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to $2$ marks.
- Selective attention <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sustained attention <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_
- Divided attention <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Focused/concentrated attention <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Switching/alternating attention <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State two characteristics of selective attention.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to $2$ marks.
- Focuses on relevant cues while ignoring distractions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Filters out irrelevant information (reduces processing of non-essential cues) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps reduce cognitive overload / frees cognitive resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves with practice and experience (more efficient cue selection) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how attentional focus influences performance in the basketball scenario.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to $3$ marks.
- Directing focus to relevant cues (e.g., defender position, teammate movement) can enhance reaction time/execution <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps prevent attention being captured by irrelevant stimuli (e.g., crowd noise, coach shouting when not actionable) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves decision-making under pressure (e.g., choosing pass vs drive vs shoot) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports accuracy/coordination of skill execution by maintaining focus on task-relevant information <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how arousal level affects attentional control, with reference to Table 1.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to $4$ marks.
- At low arousal, attention may be overly broad/inefficient, making it difficult to filter irrelevant stimuli; can contribute to slow reactions or missed key cues (as in Table 1) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Moderate arousal typically supports optimal attentional focus and performance (e.g., inverted-U), enabling prioritization of relevant cues (as in Table 1) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- At high arousal, attentional focus may narrow excessively (“tunnel vision”), causing the athlete to miss important external cues (e.g., open teammate) (as in Table 1) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Excessive arousal can also increase distractibility or shift attention internally (worry/self-focus), impairing decision-making and reaction time <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cue utilization theory may be used: increased arousal reduces the number of cues attended to, which can be helpful up to a point but detrimental when too narrow <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the role of cue utilization in effective attentional control in the basketball scenario.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to $4$ marks.
- Cue utilization is the ability to notice, select and use key/task-relevant stimuli from the environment <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps prioritize relevant information (e.g., defender spacing, teammate cut) over irrelevant information (crowd/benches) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports anticipation and timing (predicting where the screen or pass option will appear) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces information overload by limiting attention to the most diagnostic cues <marks>1 mark</marks>
- More expert performers typically use fewer but more relevant cues, improving efficiency under time pressure <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how distractions can be minimized during competitive performance in this scenario.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to $5$ marks.
- Pre-performance routines can narrow attention to controllable, task-relevant cues (benefit), but may be disrupted in chaotic game situations (limitation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Mental rehearsal/imagery can build focus routines and increase preparedness for pressure (benefit), but effectiveness varies with athlete skill in imagery (limitation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Training under simulated pressure/noise increases familiarity and improves attentional control transfer to competition (benefit), but may not replicate the full unpredictability of real opponents/crowds (limitation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Self-talk cues (instructional or motivational) can redirect attention and block distractions (benefit), but poorly chosen self-talk can increase internal focus and anxiety (limitation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Managing the environment where possible (e.g., communication signals, positioning away from distractions) can aid focus (benefit), but many external distractions cannot be removed in competition (limitation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Attentional strategies such as external focus cues (e.g., “see the gap”, “hit the target hand”) can reduce susceptibility to crowd/pressure, though over-focusing on one cue may cause missed options <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A novice golfer is learning the basic swing. They are making frequent errors, hitting the ground before the ball, and must consciously think about their grip, stance, and backswing for every shot. According to Fitts and Posner’s stages of learning, which stage of learning is being demonstrated?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.2 Attentional control > C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The golfer must consciously think about grip, stance, and backswing and is making frequent errors—characteristics of the early (cognitive) stage of skill acquisition where attentional focus is high and execution is not yet automatic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is about identifying a stage of learning within motor learning processes (skill acquisition as a learning process), which broadly fits the motor learning process content.

options.content: Cognitive
options.markscheme: Correct: in the cognitive stage, performers make frequent errors and must consciously focus on key components of the skill.

options.content: Associative
options.markscheme: Incorrect: in the associative stage, performance becomes more consistent with fewer errors and less conscious control than described.

options.content: Autonomous
options.markscheme: Incorrect: in the autonomous stage, the skill is largely automatic and requires minimal conscious attention.

options.content: Cognitive (verbal-motor)
options.markscheme: Incorrect: this is not a distinct stage from the cognitive stage in Fitts and Posner’s model; it is a synonym/variant label and would not be used as a separate option.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.2 Attentional control > C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on attentional control: strategies, focus regulation, attentional capacity under anxiety, novice vs expert attentional focus, Nideffer’s attentional styles, and training attention for consistent performance.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.4 Stress and coping > C.4.1 Arousal and anxiety > C.4.1.1 Arousal as a physical and psychological activation affecting performance
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.1.1 Arousal as a physical and psychological activation affecting performance
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Several parts explicitly address arousal’s role in focus regulation and how arousal relates to attentional control during performance.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.4 Stress and coping > C.4.1 Arousal and anxiety > C.4.1.2 Anxiety's dual effect
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.1.2 Anxiety's dual effect
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Questions on high anxiety effects on attentional capacity and anxiety’s influence on attention/performance align with anxiety’s dual effect content.

parts.markscheme:

parts.content: State two limitations of random practice for beginners.
parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Describe how modeling supports the learning of motor skills.
parts.markscheme:
**Markscheme:**

- Observing a skilled demonstration provides a visual representation of the skill <marks>A1</marks>
- Helps the learner to visualize the ideal movement or technique <marks>A1</marks>
- Enhances the formation and refinement of the motor program <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Explain the role of schema theory in developing motor programs.
parts.markscheme:
**Markscheme:**

- Schema theory involves building a set of generalized rules from practice experiences <marks>A1</marks>
- It leads to the development of generalized motor programs (GMPs) rather than specific ones for every movement <marks>A1</marks>
- It allows for the transfer of learning across different but related motor tasks <marks>A1</marks>
- It increases the learner's adaptability to novel situations or varying environmental conditions <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Outline how error detection ability changes across learning stages.
parts.markscheme:
**Markscheme:**

- Cognitive stage: the learner relies primarily on external or augmented feedback to identify errors <marks>A1</marks>
- Associative stage: the learner begins to detect and correct errors independently using intrinsic feedback <marks>A1</marks>
- Autonomous stage: the learner has a high level of self-correction and internal monitoring capability <marks>A1</marks>
- Overall, error detection becomes faster, more consistent, and more accurate as learning progresses <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Discuss the benefits of using mental rehearsal alongside physical practice.
parts.markscheme:
**Markscheme:**

- Enhances skill retention by reinforcing memory traces and cognitive elements of the task <marks>A1</marks>
- Reinforces neural pathways associated with the physical movement without causing physical fatigue <marks>A1</marks>
- Improves psychological factors such as self-confidence and focus while reducing anxiety <marks>A1</marks>
- Supports the visualization of the correct movement sequence and timing <marks>A1</marks>
- Provides a valuable alternative when physical practice is limited by time, facility availability, or injury <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

questions.specification:
The table below provides observations of an athlete's performance during a training session:

| Feature | Observation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Consistency** | Highly repeatable movement patterns with negligible variation |
| **Feedback** | Primarily relies on intrinsic kinesthetic feel to adjust movement |
| **Attention** | Able to process complex tactical information while executing the skill |

Which stage of motor learning matches the observations in the table?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.2 Attentional control > C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.2.1 Effective skill execution requires appropriate attentional focus
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The description emphasizes attentional demands (able to process complex tactical information while executing the skill), which aligns with attentional focus/automaticity in skill execution.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks to identify a stage of motor learning (cognitive/associative/autonomous), which broadly fits learning as a process of behavioral change, though the specific 'stages' framing isn’t explicitly listed as a separate leaf topic.

options.content: Cognitive stage
options.markscheme: The cognitive stage is characterized by a high frequency of errors and a heavy reliance on extrinsic feedback as the learner attempts to understand the basic mechanics of the task.

options.content: Associative stage
options.markscheme: In the associative stage, the performer is refining the movement and developing internal feedback loops, but the skill is not yet fully automatic or resistant to external distractions.

options.content: Autonomous stage
options.markscheme: The autonomous stage involves the skill being performed with minimal conscious effort, allowing the athlete to focus on environmental or tactical cues without a decrease in performance consistency.

options.content: Autonomous stage
options.markscheme: The autonomous stage involves the skill being performed with minimal conscious effort, allowing the athlete to focus on environmental or tactical cues without a decrease in performance consistency.

questions.specification: Which situation shows negative transfer of learning?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which scenario shows negative transfer of learning, which is directly part of transfer of learning within motor learning processes.

options.content: A baseball player struggles with cricket batting because of different swing mechanics
options.markscheme: Negative transfer occurs when the learning or performance of one skill hinders the performance or learning of another skill. The differing requirements for footwork and swing trajectory between baseball and cricket often lead to this interference.

options.content: A runner improves sprinting after resistance training
options.markscheme: This represents positive transfer or a training effect where physical conditioning improves performance in a related activity.

options.content: A gymnast improves beam balance after practicing yoga
options.markscheme: This is an example of positive transfer, as the balance skills developed in yoga enhance performance on the gymnastics beam.

options.content: A swimmer adapts freestyle skills to triathlon swimming
options.markscheme: This is an example of positive transfer, where existing skills are successfully applied to a new but similar environment.

questions.specification: Which statement best distinguishes performance from learning?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question contrasts performance (temporary, fluctuates day to day) with learning (relatively permanent change), which directly matches the definition of learning in motor learning processes.

options.content: Performance is relatively permanent.
Learning is temporary and fluctuates.
Performance is temporary and can vary from day to day.
Learning is influenced only by physical fitness.

questions.specification: Why can performance be an unreliable measure of learning in sport?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.1 Learning as a process leading to a relatively permanent behavioral change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question contrasts performance vs. learning, highlighting that learning is relatively permanent while performance can fluctuate due to temporary factors (e.g., fatigue, anxiety, motivation), which is central to defining learning in motor learning theory.

options.content: Because learning is a relatively permanent change in capability, whereas performance can vary from day to day.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This contrasts learning with performance, but it does not identify the key issue that performance can be affected by temporary factors.

options.content: Because performance can be influenced by temporary factors such as fatigue, anxiety, or motivation.
options.markscheme: Correct. Performance fluctuates due to temporary factors, so observed performance does not always reflect underlying learning.

options.content: Because improved performance during practice may be due to guidance or feedback, and may not be retained or transferred later.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. This is a valid reason performance may not reflect learning, but it is not the best answer compared with the broader explanation in option B.

options.content: Because performance measures are always subjective and cannot be measured consistently in sport.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Performance can often be measured objectively and reliably; unreliability as a measure of learning is mainly due to temporary influences and lack of retention/transfer evidence.

questions.specification: Which statement about the psychological refractory period is correct?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.2 The psychological refractory period and its impact on reaction time
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.2 The psychological refractory period and its impact on reaction time
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which statement correctly describes the psychological refractory period, a motor learning concept explaining delayed response to a second stimulus when the first is still being processed.

options.content: It makes athletes respond more quickly to multiple stimuli.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. The psychological refractory period (PRP) describes a delay in responding to a second stimulus when processing of the first is still ongoing, not faster responses to multiple stimuli.

options.content: It occurs when the brain processes two stimuli simultaneously without delay.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. PRP occurs because processing two stimuli at once creates a processing bottleneck, producing a delay rather than no delay.

options.content: It delays the response to a second stimulus if the first is still being processed.
options.markscheme: Correct. PRP is the delay in reaction time to a second stimulus when it follows closely after a first stimulus that is still being processed (dual-task processing bottleneck).

options.content: It increases the delay in responding to a second stimulus by diverting attention to irrelevant cues.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Although irrelevant cues can reduce performance via distraction, PRP specifically refers to the delay caused by processing limitations when two stimuli are presented in quick succession.

questions.specification: Which best explains why transfer of learning can be positive in some cases and negative in others?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.2 Motor learning > C.2.1 Motor learning processes > C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.2.1.3 Transfer of learning and its influence on skill acquisition
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks why transfer of learning can be positive or negative, which depends on similarity or conflict between an old and new skill—core to the concept of transfer of learning in motor learning.

options.content: It depends on whether the performer is at a beginner or advanced stage of learning.
options.markscheme: Stage of learning can influence how quickly a skill is acquired, but positive/negative transfer is primarily explained by how similar or conflicting the skills are.

options.content: It depends on the similarity or conflict between the old and new skill.
options.markscheme: Positive transfer is more likely when skills share similar movement patterns/perceptual demands; negative transfer occurs when previously learned patterns interfere due to conflict.

options.content: It depends mainly on how much practice was completed in the original skill, regardless of the new skill.
options.markscheme: Amount of practice alone does not determine the direction (positive vs negative) of transfer; the relationship between the skills (similarity/conflict) is key.

options.content: It depends on whether the original skill was learned first through observational learning rather than physical practice.
options.markscheme: Observational learning can contribute to skill acquisition, but it does not explain why transfer becomes positive or negative; this depends on similarity/interference between skills.
